name: Homewood Housing Circle: Community Renewal & Affordable Housing Platform
place: Homewood, a historically Black neighborhood in Pittsburgh marked by deep roots and decades of disinvestment
tagline: Built the rails for community housing, but never ran the round
whyItMattered: Homewood has trusted institutions and people doing the work, but no shared system tying participation, funding, and ownership together. The intent was to keep long-time residents in place through housing repair and community-directed reinvestment.
story: Homewood Children's Village is a long-standing anchor in a neighborhood with strong leadership and a history of collective action, but fragmented systems for housing, funding, and decision-making. The plan was a community housing grant round: repair microgrants, block-level rehab, and affordability upgrades, prioritized by community voting.

After the grant landed, the team built infrastructure first. They stood up a 2-of-3 multisig, deployed an MVP platform called The Village for treasury and participation tracking, and drafted a legal framework for compliant community-owned assets.

Land acquisition was initiated with the Urban Redevelopment Authority and the Homewood Collaborative, putting two parcels into escrow. But the housing grant round itself never ran: no open call, no applicants, no community vote, no microgrants.
The team was candid that real-world land acquisition was the bottleneck, and that going straight to distributions might have served the community sooner.

signatureStory.title: A foundation, not yet a home
signatureStory.text: The chosen story is the platform build itself: smart contracts for treasury and governance, a web app for onboarding and participation tracking, designed alongside HCV's Check & Connect program. The team's own words capture both the ambition and the gap. The infrastructure exists; the housing impact does not yet.
signatureStory.quote: It turned an idea into something that can actually be used.

delivered: Stood up a real 2-of-3 Gnosis Safe multisig and moved grant funds on-chain, with a fully traceable, now-empty wallet
Deployed an MVP platform (The Village) with treasury, governance, and participation-tracking components
Produced a legal and compliance framework for community-based tokenized ownership
Initiated land acquisition with the URA and Homewood Collaborative; two parcels reportedly in escrow (not closed)
Ran one workshop with Check & Connect students and coordinated with the HCV board
No open call, no applications, no community voting, and no housing microgrants; $0 reached an identifiable local housing project

outcomes.text: A working infrastructure layer (multisig, MVP, legal scaffolding) now exists that could support a housing model later
Alignment between HCV and a coordination model for participation, funding, and decisions
A pathway to asset-backed community participation, conditional on closing the parcels in escrow
Housing repaired, residents supported, or microgrants distributed — contingent on new funding, none yet confirmed

status: Infrastructure built; the funded housing grant round did not happen

verdict.financial: Fully traceable on-chain, but the local-project disbursement claim is contradicted
verdict.line: Needs Follow-Up: the grant is 100% on-chain-traceable and the wallet is empty, but none of it reached a local housing project, and the pivot from a housing grant round to a platform build is not disclosed.

honesty: This program built genuine technical infrastructure and kept an exemplary, fully transparent money trail, but it did not run the housing grant round it was funded for: no open call, no community vote, and $0 to any local housing project. Spend went to platform development, an off-chain land escrow that partly round-tripped to the co-funder, legal, and gas, and that change of direction was framed as delay rather than disclosed as a pivot.

proposed.summary: Run a community housing grant round to keep long-time Homewood residents in place.
points: Fund 2 to 10 local housing repair, rehab, and affordability projects
Prioritize grants through HCV-permissioned community voting
Distribute capital on-chain via stablecoin disbursements and attestations
Direct 70% of the budget to local housing grants

funding.matchNote: $9,000 of the co-funding round-tripped back to the co-funder's own address, and the application's named foundation partners never appear on-chain — the sole co-funder is an unidentified 'Angel Source' wallet.

disbursements.purpose: Land-acquisition escrow for parcels; the $9,000 leg round-tripped to the co-funder's own address. Not a local-project grant.
Developmental support for the platform build; forwarded onward to a developer contract. Operations, not a local-project grant.
Legal work on structuring and compliance; forwarded onward to the co-funder's address.
Gas, bridging, and transaction fees for on-chain operations.

stories.title: Building the rails before the round
stories.text: After funding landed, the team built infrastructure first: a 2-of-3 Gnosis Safe multisig, an MVP platform called The Village for treasury, governance, and participation tracking, and a legal framework for compliant community-owned assets, designed alongside HCV's Check & Connect program.
stories.quote: It turned an idea into something that can actually be used.

stories.title: Parcels in escrow, a round never run
stories.text: Land acquisition was initiated with the Urban Redevelopment Authority and the Homewood Collaborative, putting two parcels into escrow. But the housing grant round itself never happened: no open call, no applicants, no community vote, and no microgrants reached a local project.
stories.quote: Parcels are now in escrow, establishing a concrete asset base for the system.

stories.title: Candid about the bottleneck
stories.text: The team named real-world land acquisition as the bottleneck, with escrow, approvals, and public-agency coordination taking far longer than expected. They reflected that going straight to Check & Connect distributions might have served the community sooner.
stories.quote: We would have taken more steps in the initial RWA acquisition or went straight to Check and Connect distributions to accelerate the process.

dimensionEvidence.evidence: A real 2-of-3 Gnosis Safe multisig, a deployed MVP (treasury, governance, participation tracking), on-chain fund movement, and a legal/compliance scaffold. Technology meaningfully supported coordination and transparency, though there is no community uptake yet and the community is not actively transacting.

Genuine relationship and coordination work: the HCV board, the Homewood Collaborative, and Hajj Media, plus one workshop with Check & Connect students. Integration into existing program infrastructure rather than parallel systems, though it stops short of durable mutual-support structures.

Local economic effect was minimal: no funds circulated to local housing projects, businesses, or residents. The asset-backed participation model is defined but not activated; the largest spend went to a developer and the rest to land escrow.

correlation.claim: Round status: launched and completed, all funds distributed
correlation.reality: The wallet is empty, but no grant round to local projects occurred; 'changed direction' was the accurate, unchecked box.
$0 reached an identifiable local housing project; $10,000 went to undefined off-chain land-acquisition escrow EOAs.

correlation.claim: Funded to run a community housing micro-grant round
correlation.reality: No open call, no applicants, no community voting, and no housing microgrants were run; the round never happened.

correlation.claim: Changes from plan framed as delay in the report
correlation.reality: The pivot from a housing grant round to a platform build was not disclosed as a change of direction.
Ethereum functioned as a custody rail with most funds paid off-chain: 0 attestations, minimal on-chain activity, no community transacting — the evidence supports 'Peripheral', not 'Meaningful'.

timeline.what: Application submitted to Localism Fund Round 01
Co-funding and Localism Fund grant received on-chain
Multisig stood up; platform development begins
Two land parcels moved into escrow
Housing grant round never run; no microgrants

resources.label: Legal & compliance framework for community-based tokenization

operatorChecks: ! Who controls the two escrow EOAs, and what did the $10,000 land-acquisition payment actually purchase?
! Why did the $9,000 escrow leg and the $970 legal leg round-trip back to the co-funder's own address?
! The $9,930 'Dr. Ears' payment was developer operations, not a local-project disbursement — please confirm so it can be reclassified.
Please confirm the program should be described as a platform build rather than a completed housing grant round — no open call or community vote occurred.
Can you substantiate the '27 new wallets' claim and the 'Meaningful' Ethereum rating? Otherwise both will be dropped or hedged.
! Did any of the application's named co-funders — R.K. Mellon, Heinz Endowments, Hillman — materialize? The sole on-chain co-funder is an unidentified 'Angel Source' wallet.
! Your report's spend categories sum to $21,100 against the $21,000 total — please reconcile.
fundingNote: The $11,200 allocation arrived as ~$10,989 USDC net of bridge fees. Recorded disbursements moved fully on-chain and are traceable, but none reached a local housing project: the $10,000 'disbursed to local projects' went to off-chain land-acquisition escrow (with $9,000 round-tripping to the co-funder), the largest spend was a developer pass-through, and 0% funded the promised housing microgrants.
